document.title: Accuracy of Portable Chest Radiography in the Critical Care Setting: Diagnosis of Pneumonia Based on Quantitative Cultures Obtained From Protected Brush Catheter

sourceSummary.oneLine: Clinical literature article evaluating portable chest radiography accuracy for predicting nosocomial pneumonia in critical care patients using protected brush catheter quantitative cultures.
sourceSummary.officialUse: May support clinical evaluation discussion of diagnostic limitations of portable chest radiography in critical care settings.
keyPoints: Sixty-six supine portable chest radiographs from 62 critical care patients suspected of pneumonia were examined by two blinded radiologists.
Protected brush catheter quantitative culture results were compared with chest radiograph scores.
The authors concluded that portable chest radiograph in the critical care setting is not accurate in predicting pneumonia when diagnosis is based on quantitative culture of PBC specimens.

concepts.name: Portable chest radiography
concepts.evidence: We conclude the portable chest radiograph in the critical care setting is not accurate in predicting the presence of pneumonia

concepts.name: Quantitative cultures
concepts.evidence: Quantitative culture results obtained from protected brush catheter (PBC) specimens were compared with chest radiograph scores.

concepts.name: Nosocomial pneumonia
concepts.evidence: The purpose of this study was to examine the diagnostic accuracy of the portable chest radiograph in predicting the presence of nosocomial pneumonia

concepts.name: Critical care setting
concepts.evidence: Accuracy of Portable Chest Radiography in the Critical Care Setting

evidence.claim: The article studied portable chest radiography accuracy in the critical care setting for pneumonia diagnosis.
evidence.evidenceText: Accuracy of Portable Chest Radiography in the Critical Care Setting Diagnosis of Pneumonia Based on Quantitative Cultures Obtained From Protected Brush Catheter

evidence.claim: The study included 66 supine portable chest radiographs from 62 critical care unit patients suspected of pneumonia.
evidence.evidenceText: Sixty-six supine portable chest radiographs done on the day of bronchoscopy in 62 critical care unit patients suspected of having pneumonia were examined in a blinded fashion by two radiologists.

evidence.claim: Chest radiograph scores were compared with quantitative culture results from protected brush catheter specimens.
evidence.evidenceText: Quantitative culture results obtained from protected brush catheter (PBC) specimens were compared with chest radiograph scores.

evidence.claim: For one observer, reported chest radiograph performance included sensitivity 0.60, specificity 0.29, overall agreement 0.41, positive predictive value 0.34, and negative predictive value 0.55.
evidence.evidenceText: For one observer, the sensitivity of the chest radiograph for predicting the presence of positive culture results was 0.60, specificity was 0.29, overall agreement was 0.41, positive predictive value was 0.34, and negative predictive value was 0.55.

evidence.claim: For the second observer, reported values included sensitivity 0.64, specificity 0.27, overall agreement 0.41, positive predictive value 0.35, and negative predictive value 0.55.
evidence.evidenceText: For the second observer, the values were as follows: sensitivity, 0.64; specificity, 0.27; overall agreement, 0.41; positive predictive value, 0.35; and negative predictive value, 0.55.

evidence.claim: Interobserver reproducibility was marginal, with kappa statistic 0.27.
evidence.evidenceText: The kappa statistic was calculated at 0.27 indicating marginal interobserver reproducibility.

evidence.claim: The authors concluded that portable chest radiograph was not accurate for predicting pneumonia when diagnosis was based on quantitative cultures from PBC specimens.
evidence.evidenceText: We conclude the portable chest radiograph in the critical care setting is not accurate in predicting the presence of pneumonia when the diagnosis is based on quantitative cultures obtained from protected brush catheter specimens.

evidence.claim: Portable chest radiographs were obtained on the day of bronchoscopy before bronchoscopy using portable x-ray equipment.
evidence.evidenceText: A portable chest radiograph was obtained in all patients on the day of the bronchoscopy prior to the bronchoscopy. The radiographs were done with the patient in a supine position using portable x-ray equipment

evidence.claim: The article appeared in CHEST volume 105 issue 3 in March 1994.
